coredata.dc:title: Dual anticancer drug delivery of D-galactose-functionalized stimuli-responsive nanogels for targeted therapy of the liver hepatocellular carcinoma
coredata.dc:creator: Pooresmaeil, Malihe
Namazi, Hassan
Salehi, Roya
coredata.dc:description: In the current research work for the first time, we designed and fabricated a new double-responsive (pH and temperature) and photoluminescent nanogels capped with D-galactose (D-Gal) moieties (CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal) in three steps and then evaluated its potential as a targeted drug carrier. The used common characterization techniques approved the success in fabrication of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels. CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels demonstrated homogeneous size distribution with an average hydrodynamic diameter of about 657 nm. About 47.8% and 72% of loading capacity respectively were observed respectively for methotrexate (MTX) and doxorubicin (DOX) anticancer drugs (MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal). The controlled drug release ability of the MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal was verified through a comparison of the drug release profiles at the simulated tumor and physiological conditions. In vitro cytotoxicity, 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) staining, and cell cycle assays displayed the good biocompatibility of CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal. Furthermore, fluorescent imaging approved its bioimaging potential. Overall summation of the achieved results proposes that the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal can be generally tested as a medical nanovehicle with a wide range of applicability potential for cancer cells monitoring and co-drug delivery.

head.ce:title: Dual anticancer drug delivery of D-galactose-functionalized stimuli-responsive nanogels for targeted therapy of the liver hepatocellular carcinoma

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current research work for the first time, we designed and fabricated a new double-responsive (pH and temperature) and photoluminescent nanogels capped with D-galactose (D-Gal) moieties (CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal) in three steps and then evaluated its potential as a targeted drug carrier. The used common characterization techniques approved the success in fabrication of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels. CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels demonstrated homogeneous size distribution with an average hydrodynamic diameter of about 657 nm. About 47.8% and 72% of loading capacity respectively were observed respectively for methotrexate (MTX) and doxorubicin (DOX) anticancer drugs (MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal). The controlled drug release ability of the MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal was verified through a comparison of the drug release profiles at the simulated tumor and physiological conditions. In vitro cytotoxicity, 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) staining, and cell cycle assays displayed the good biocompatibility of CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal. Furthermore, fluorescent imaging approved its bioimaging potential. Overall summation of the achieved results proposes that the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal can be generally tested as a medical nanovehicle with a wide range of applicability potential for cancer cells monitoring and co-drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, hepatocellular cancer is the third leading reason for all tumor deaths worldwide, with further than half a million new patients being diagnosed every year [1–3]. Chemotherapy is one of the mainstays of treatment of this type of cancer. This treatment procedure mainly relies on broad-spectrum anticancer drugs which DOX and MTX are two widely-used and well-known chemotherapy agents [4–7]. However, direct clinical administration of the anticancer drugs is commonly accompanied by drawbacks like the metabolism of the anticancer drugs by normal cells besides tumor cells and also the uncontrolled drug distribution [8–12]. On the other hand, compared with single-drug therapy, combination therapy can maximize the therapeutic influence [13].

Different drug delivery systems were designed and prepared for dual anticancer drug delivery to reduce the side effects of anticancer drugs and meet this demand [14–16]. In the meantime, the high surface-to-volume ratio and nanometric size of the nanoparticulate-based drug delivery systems favorite them with exceptional technical advantages [17–22]. Among the designed drug carriers based on nanomaterials, recently, more biocompatible carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with a size below 10 nm (nm) have attracted much more attention for anticancer drug delivery applications [20,23–25]. Suitable photo-stability, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, simple synthesis route, physical and chemical stability, water solubility, and also environmentally friendly are some advantages of the CQDs [8,26]. In the structure of these “zero-dimensional” carbon nanomaterial, the different types of oxygen-containing functional groups are placed on the surface. Moreover, the present sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the structure of CQDs can provide the possibility of the π − π interaction with the drug molecules with aromatic structures [27]. However, the tendency for aggregation of these nanomaterials in dry states is considered to be a limiting issue that could influence their performance [28]. Incorporation of the CQDs into appropriate matrices, in particular, nanogels could be considered as a useful procedure for solving this.

Nanogels are nano-sized hydrogels that received growing attention in recent years. These nanomaterials can merge the features of hydrogels and nanomaterials [29,30]. Nanogels have been prepared from different raw materials, in between the nanogels made from stimuli-responsive components have been recognized as one of the most versatile systems [31]. Poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) is considered one of the most promising pH-responsive polymers which are commonly used for site-specific drug delivery [32]. On the other hand among the thermo-sensitive polymer family, N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAAm) based systems are widely evaluated because this polymer displays phase transition temperature close to the human body temperature. Furthermore, the lower critical solution temperature (LCST) of PNIPAM can be easily adjusted to the desired values via the incorporation of the new hydrophilic portions [33]. For example when the hydrophilic saccharides are introduced into the NIPAM based systems the LCST shifts to a higher temperature [34]. However, PNIPAm is non-biodegradable, therefore, the biodegradable thermoresponsive systems can be achieved by incorporation of biodegradable segments or its copolymerization with monomers possessing a hydrolytic side chain. For example, the introduction of β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD) can increase the biodegradability of the system and made it possible to be hydrolysis [35].

β-CD is one member of the torus-shaped cyclic oligosaccharide family composed of seven D (+)-glucose units which are bonded by α-1,4-linkages [36–40]. In the structure of this cyclic oligosaccharide, the exterior surface is hydrophilic however the internal cavity is hydrophobic that enabling it to form the complex with the non-polar molecules [41]. Besides these, nanogels could be conjugated with bioactive targeting molecules to gain the targeted anticancer drug delivery [4,42]. D-Gal is a monosaccharide with the same chemical formula and structure as glucose, i.e., C6H12O6. The difference only is in one of hydroxyl group positions that leads to the unlike biochemical and chemical properties. This monosaccharide has been recognized as one main liver-specific targeting for cancer therapy because of its affinity to asialoglycoprotein receptor (ASGP-R) [43,44].

Up to now, various studies were accomplished to assess the ability of the nanogels for dual anticancer drug delivery. However, our survey on the published literature presented that, until today, no research work has been published on the synthesis of CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels as a carrier for DOX and MTX. In fact, the key objective of the present investigation is introducing the photoluminescent CQDs and D-Gal the carrier structure to obtain the new system having both bioimaging and targeted drug delivery potential. Therefore, by considering the above-mentioned facts the present research work aimed at constructing a new targeted and smart nanocarrier based on a green and economical strategy to produce the high-luminescence intelligent CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA nanogels which was then covalently functionalized with D-Gal as a targeted ligand. The fabricated CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels were characterized in detail. Additionally, dual anticancer drug delivery, biocompatibility, cellular uptake, cell cycle, and bioimaging were explored for the prepared nanosystem. Based on the outcome of this work it has been believed that the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal can be an appropriate candidate for targeted and stimuli-responsive anticancer delivery to human hepatoma G2 (HepG2) cells; human liver cancer cell line.

D-Gal, hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), N,N′-methylenbisacrylamid (MBA), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), AA, potassium persulfate (KPS), dimethylformamide (DMF), and methane sulfonic acid were products from Merck Co. β-CD, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), NIPAM, 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), and DAPI were provided from Sigma-Aldrich. The liver cancer (HepG2) and normal human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) cell lines were supplied from the Pasteur Institute of Iran and used for cellular tests. MTX and DOX respectively were achieved from Zahravi Pharmaceutical Co. (Iran) and Sobhan Pharmaceuticals Co. (Iran). Trypsin, fetal bovine serum albumin (FBS), Roswell park memorial institute 1640 growth medium (RPMI), fetal bovine serum (FBS), ribonuclease (RnaseA), and penicillin–streptomycin were obtained from Gibco (USA). The double-distilled water was utilized overall in this work.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the samples were taken in a Bruker Instruments (model Aquinox 55, Germany) with potassium bromide (KBr) tablets. In this technique, the spectra were achieved between 4000 and 400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and an average of 150 scans. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy studies were carried out on a Shimadzu 1700 spectrophotometer. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) measurements were performed with a DSC instrument (Linseis-L63/45, Germany) using air as a carrier gas. In the DSC test, the samples with a weight of ∼ 10 mg were treated from ambient temperature up to 50 °C. Thermal stability was assessed by thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) using Thermo-Gravimetric Analyzer (Linseis STA-PT 1000) in a range of 25–600 °C, with a ramp of 10 °C/min The samples were visualized by transition electron microscopy (TEM) (LEO 906E transmission electron microscope). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs were obtained using a TESCANMIRA3 scanning electron microscope operating at 5 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were recorded on a Nanosurf Mobile S in the tapping mode. Photoluminescent (PL) measurements were done employing a Jasco FP-8300 spectrofluorimeter (Japan). Hydrodynamic size and zeta potential were measured using a dynamic light scattering (DLS) particle size and zeta potential (ZP) analyzer (Microtrac, model Nanotrac Wave).

The procedure for the synthesis of CQDs from D-Gal was based on the previously reported research for the CQDs synthesis with minor changes [45]. Generally, an aqueous solution of D-Gal (10 mL, 1 M) was mixed with HCl (5 mL, 37%) and then the reaction mixture was refluxed for 1 h at 80 °C. The resulting CQDs solution was neutralized with NaOH (1 M), filtered, and eventually dialyzed against deionized water for 24 h. Dialyzed medium changed every 4 h. In the following, the solvent was evaporated under reduced pressure. The produced pure CQDs were dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C and then stored within a dark vial.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of photoluminescent, temperature, and pH-sensitive core–shell nanogels
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, the nanogels were prepared through the free radical crosslinking in water as a green solvent. Firstly, MBA (13.86 mg, 0.090 mmol) was dissolved in 50 mL of water under magnetic agitation. Then the argon gas was set up and CQDs (20 wt%), β-CD (204.200 mg, 0.180 mmol), NIPAM (366.600 mg, 3.240 mmol), and SDS (10.600 mg, 0.036 mmol) were added to the reaction flask. The temperature of the reaction was adjusted at 70 °C and KPS (27.000 mg, 0.099 mmol) was slowly poured into the mixture. Polymerization was initiated and after 30 min the AA (25.800 mg, 0.358 mmol) was injected into the reaction balloon slowly. The reaction was continued for 7 h in the previous condition. The formed CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA nanogels were recovered by centrifuge and dialyzed against water for 72 h to remove the formed linear polymers and also the unreacted raw materials. The dialysis water was exchanged four times every day. The CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA nanogels were freeze-dried and then stored in a desiccator at room temperature for further analyses as well as usage [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of D-Gal moieties to CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA nanogels (CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal)
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, for functionalization of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA hybrid nanogels with D-Gal, firstly the D-Gal (20 mg, 0.112 mmol) was poured in 10 mL of DMF. In the next step, the methane sulfonic acid (0.004 mL, 0.056 mmol) and CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA nanogels (200 mg) respectively were added into the reaction medium. The temperature of the reaction was set at 60 °C and the reaction was followed for 24 h at this temperature. Finally, the produced CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels were collected, washed, and dried under reduced pressure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro study of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal biodegradation
ce:section.ce:para:
In vitro biodegradation property of the fabricated CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels was evaluated in the presence of lysozyme at physiological pH. In this regard, the dried CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels were placed in a glass centrifuge vial containing lysozyme (PBS, 10 mL, pH 7.4) and incubated on a shaker incubator at 37 °C. At predetermined times, the mixture was centrifuged, washed, dried in the vacuum, and then weighted to analyze the degradation behavior. Finally, the initial and final weights were utilized to obtain the remaining weight percentage via Eq. (1).

In Eq. (1) the Wt and W0 symbols respectively rely on the remaining weight of each vial plus degraded nanogels after reported time and the original weight of each vial plus nanogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro evaluation of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal colloidal stability
ce:section.ce:para: The colloidal stability of CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels was explored during 3 days to further evaluate its potential as a drug carrier from the stability and aggregation viewpoints in the simulated environment with the application medium. In this regard, a suspension of CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels was prepared with the same concentration (0.1 mg/mL) in four different mediums; water, PBS (pH 7.4), FBS, and complete culture medium (RPMI 1640 containing FBS (10% v/v)). Eventually, the stability was examined visually through observation of the turbidity changes in each solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTX and DOX co-delivery via CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels
MTX and DOX co-loading in CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels
ce:section.ce:para:
DOX@MTX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels were prepared via sequential loading of MTX and DOX on CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal. In this way, Briefly, a definite amount of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels were immersed in MTX solution (5 mL, 1000 ppm) and then the mixture mild shaking was accompanied by a shaker at room temperature. After 24 h, the mixture was centrifuged to harvest the MTX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels, as well as, they were washed with PBS for the elimination of the unloaded MTX drug molecules. The remaining concentration of MTX in the drug loading solution was determined employing a UV–Vis spectrophotometer and used for the determination of the unloaded MTX concentration. In the following, the MTX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal was dispersed in DOX solution (5 mL, 1000 ppm) and shaken was accompanied for 24 h at room temperature. In the next step, the mixture was centrifuged to collect the MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels, as well as, they were washed with PBS for the elimination of the unloaded DOX drug molecules. The remaining concentration of DOX in the drug loading solution was determined employing a UV–Vis spectrophotometer and used for specifying the unloaded DOX concentration. Eventually, the obtained drug amounts in each step were utilized to calculate drug loading (%) by considering the volume of the drug loading solution as well as the initial weight of each drug.

Both DOX and MTX release behaviors were investigated for DOX@MTX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels over 72 h in two different conditions; (pH 7.4, 37 °C) and (pH 5.0, 40 °C) which respectively simulate the normal and tumor cells conditions. Briefly, an aliquot of drug-loaded nanogels (20 mg in 3 mL) was added into the dialysis bag and then the collection was suspended in 7 mL of release medium under gentle shaking. At predefined time intervals, 3 mL of release medium was withdrawn and an equivalent volume of the fresh buffer was substituted with it. Finally, the release quantity of both drugs was specified employing UV–Vis spectroscopy at maximum absorbance, calibration curves, and Eq. (4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release kinetics study
ce:section.ce:para:
The four most common mathematical models; zero-order, first-order, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer-Peppas models were used to explore the kinetics and mechanism of drug release from DOX@MTX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels. In this regard, the obtained experimental drug release data were fitted with linear form of mentioned models and the calculated R2 values determined the well-fitting of experimental data with a certain studied kinetic model. So, the higher value of R2 implies more compliance with desired models. The linear equation formula of each studied kinetic model is reported below.
(5)

In Eqs. (5)–(8) the k0, k1, kH, and kP respectively denote the rate constants of the zero-order, first-order, Korsmeyer-Peppas, and Higuchi kinetic models. As well as in these equations the Q is the amount of the released drug molecules at time t. In the end, the determined diffusional exponent “n” value was utilized for clarifying the mechanism of drug release. So, n = 1, 0.45 < n > 1, and n ≤ 0.45 respectively illustrate that the drug release from the carriers occurs via a case-II transport, non-Fickian transport, and Fickian diffusion mechanism.

Cytotoxicity assay was done to evaluate the biocompatibility of the neat CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels as well as its cancer cells killing capability after dual drug loading. Generally, at first, the HUVEC and HepG2 cell lines were seeded in 96-well plates and allowed to adhere overnight. Afterward, the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels, DOX@MTX, and DOX@MTX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels were added to 96-well plates in different concentrations and incubated for 48 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2 atmosphere. Following the time of incubation, the unused medium was drained and the MTT solution (200 μL, 5 mg/mL) was added to the wells. The cells were incubated for 4 h in the previous condition. Thereafter the medium was aspirated and 200 μL of DMSO solution was added to the wells. After 15 min, the absorbance for each well was obtained under a wavelength of 570 nm with a reference wavelength of 630 nm using the ELISA plate reader to perform the cell viability calculations.

The uptake of DOX@MTX and DOX@MTX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels by HepG2 cells was analyzed by both fluorescent microscopy and flow cytometry techniques. Briefly, for fluorescence microscopy imaging the HepG2 cells were seeded in 6-well plates containing sterile coverslips and then treated with the samples. After 3 h, the uptake of the samples by HepG2 cancer cells was observed using fluorescence microscopy (Olympus microscope Bh2-RFCA, Japan).

For flow cytometry, the HepG2 cells were seeded into 6-well plates, and the collection was incubated for 24 h. In the following, the cultured cells were treated with the samples and incubated for 3 at 37 °C. Eventually, all cell samples were trypsinized, harvested, washed with PBS, and tested by a flow cytometer (Becton Dickinson Immunocytometry Systems, San Jose, CA, USA).

Fluorescence microscopy was used to evaluate the inherent bioimaging potential of the fabricated CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels. In this regard, HepG2 cells were seeded in 6-well plates containing sterile coverslips and then incubated overnight to make the cells completely adhere to the well. In the following, the cells were treated with CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels. After 0.5 h, 1 h, and 3 h of incubation the cells were rinsed with PBS and then observed and photographed by using fluorescence microscopy (Olympus microscope Bh2-RFCA, Japan).

DAPI staining test was accomplished to study the safety, fragmentation, and condensation of cells nuclei as the result of apoptosis. Typically, selected HepG2 cells were seeded on 6-well plates containing sterile coverslips and afterward incubated at 37 °C overnight. The adhered cells were treated with CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal, DOX@MTX, and DOX@MTX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels for 48 h. In the following, the cells were rinsed with fresh PBS and fixed with 2000 µL of paraformaldehyde (3 %v/v) at room temperature for 10 min. The cells were rinsed with PBS for three rounds and then permeabilized with Triton X-100. The permeabilized HepG2 cells were washed with PBS, stained with DAPI, and eventually were visualized employing a fluorescence microscope (Olympus microscope Bh2-RFCA, Japan).

To probe the influence of CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal, DOX@MTX, and DOX@MTX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels on HepG2 cell cycle pattern, the selected cells were seeded within a 6-well plate. After 24 h, the adhered cells were treated with the studied samples and thereafter were incubated for another 48 h. The cells were rinsed with PBS, trypsinized, and then the contents of the wells were transferred to the glass vials to centrifuge. In the next step, the collected cells were fixed in cold ethanol (70%) and stored at 4 °C for 72 h. After this time the supernatant of each microtube was discharged via centrifuging and the RnaseA and PI solution was added to stain the cells. In the end, the cells were analyzed by flow cytometry (Becton Dickinson Immunocytometry Systems, San Jose, CA, USA).
For all cellular tests, the untreated cells were named as a control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and fabrication of CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels
ce:section.ce:para: Carbohydrate-based nanogels are one of the biocompatible and effective nanovehicles in anticancer drug delivery. Therefore, the current research work demonstrates a green, simple, and convenient strategy for the stimuli-responsive nanogels fabrication via chemical crosslinking. In this regard, at first, the CQDs were prepared from the D-Gal in aqueous media. Subsequently, the mixture of CQDs, NIPAM, β-CD, and then AA were chemically crosslinked via the facile free radical polymerization technique. Finally, the carboxylic acid groups on the external surface of CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA nanogels provided the opportunity to create novel nanogels for targeted therapy of the liver hepatocellular carcinoma as a result of functionalization of them with D-Gal; a targeted monosaccharide based ligand. A schematic view of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels fabrication procedure is shown in scheme 1 . After detailed characterization, the capability of the prepared CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels that benefit from the chemistry of all five portions; β-CD, NIPAM, AA, D-Gal, and CQDs was investigated from both in vitro anticancer drug delivery and cellular study viewpoints.

The possible interactions could be observed via a survey of the changes in the type and position of the functional groups of FT-IR spectra. Therefore, the structure of the crosslinked CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels and co-drug loading on it is firstly characterized by the FT-IR analysis, as shown in Fig. 1 . The FT-IR spectrum of the pure D-Gal in KBr respectively showed the characteristic bands of the hydroxyl groups, stretching vibration of CO, and also deformation vibration of C-H respectively with maxima at 3100–3400 cm−1, 1060 cm−1, and bands with medium intensity at 956 cm−1, 837 cm−1, and 770 cm−1. The as-synthesized CQDs displayed the stretching vibrations of OH and CO groups as a wide and strong band at 3353 cm−1 and 1145 cm−1 respectively. Moreover in this spectrum, the detected characteristic peaks at 1640 cm−1, 1421 cm−1, as well as the observed band at 2931 cm−1 respectively arise from the stretching vibration of CO, CC, and CH. The observed difference between the FT-IR spectrum of these two investigated materials could be a sign of the successful CQDs synthesis from the D-Gal precursor. In the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA nanogels FT-IR spectrum, besides the FT-IR bands related to the CQDs, the new main peaks were observed at 3427 cm−1 and 1724 cm−1 which could be attributed to the COOH group of polymerized AA. Additionally in this spectrum, the absorbance of the amide carbonyl groups related to the PNIPAM, bending vibration of amide N-H, and the symmetric and asymmetric stretching of C-H in methyl and methylene groups of PNIPAM appeared at 1644 cm−1, 1538 cm−1, and also as a dual adsorption band at 2925 cm−1 and 2861 cm−1. In this FT-IR spectrum, the characteristic peak of the glucose units of β-CD was also detected at ∼1037 cm−1 which originates from the COC vibrations. When CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA nanogels were reacted with D-Gal, the reaction between the carboxylic group of CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA nanogels and the hydroxyl group of D-Gal leads to the formation of an ester linkage. A partial shift in the carbonyl stretching (CO), as well as a decrease in the COOH stretching vibration intensity may be the proof for successful functionalization of CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA nanogels with D-Gal [47]. In the following, the MTX and DOX drug molecules loading on CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels was assessed via FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1b). The FT-IR spectrum of DOX showed several absorption bands at 889 cm−1, 1117 cm−1, 1724 cm−1, 2920 cm−1, and 3422 cm−1 that respectively associated the vibrations of NH, COC, CO, CH, and OH. In the FT-IR spectrum of MTX, the absorption peaks of the NH2 stretching vibration, antisymmetric mode of carboxylate units, and CN stretching vibration of primary and tertiary amines groups appeared respectively at 3386 cm−1, 1602–1560 cm−1, 1203 cm−1, and 1096 cm−1. In comparison with CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels, the FT-IR spectrum of dual anticancer drugs-loaded CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels shows some new additional absorption bands corresponding to the vibration of DOX or MTX which can verify the co-drugs loading on it. Taken together, the FT-IR analysis output could approve the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA nanogels preparation, conjugation with D-Gal, and finally co-drug loading on it.

The synthesized CQDs were further characterized employing UV–Vis spectrophotometry (Fig. 2 a). The achieved UV–Vis spectra of the CQDs revealed two absorption bands respectively at 221 nm and 304 nm. The first absorption band at 221 nm arises from the π-π* electron transition of CC linkage (sp2 domains). While the second absorption band at 304 nm corresponds to the n-π* electron transition (non-bonding oxygen states). Compared to the neat CQDs the fabricated hybrid CQDs/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels displayed a similar absorption peak, indicating that the CQDs successfully were dispersed within the nanogel networks. The lowering in intensity of the observed peak at 231 nm may be related to the involvement of the electronic conjugation during the radical polymerization reaction [48].

In the following the PL property of the synthesized CQDs, as well as the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels was investigated after excitation at 365 nm to examine and approve its bioimaging potential. Fig. 2b shows the regarded spectra. The maximum emission of both produced neat CQDs and CDs/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels appeared at ∼ 440 nm. Moreover, in comparison with CQDs, the fabricated CDs/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels displayed a broader peak. The observed partial shift and broadening can be related to the made modifications and possible filter effect, internal or external transformations, self-absorbing, and intersystem transitions that could be occurred [49]. For further confirmation, the digital image of CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels aqueous dispersion in a medium without UV lamination as well as under UV light is obtained and presented inset of Fig. 2b. As it can be seen from the images, the aqueous dispersion of the synthesized CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal is able to emit the light after excitation at 365 nm which is in good agreement with the PL analysis results. In general, the detected good PL property can introduce the synthesized CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels as a suitable candidate for bioimaging applications.

TGA test was performed on CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels and the achieved plot is shown in Fig. 2c. In the obtained thermogram the whole procedure of the thermal weight loss process can be divided into two areas. (I); loss of the adsorbed water when until the temperature of about 100 °C. (II); the decomposition of organic portions like functional monomer, CQDs, and cross-linking agent at a temperature range of 210–408 °C. As can be seen from the results, the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels were successfully fabricated in a simple way.

For thermoresponsive therapeutic delivery, it is an important issue to adjust the LCST of the used drug carrier close to body temperature. PNIPAM is one of the main well-known temperature-responsive polymers that present a reversible temperature-sensitive phase transition in neat and composed forms. Therefore, in this research work, CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels containing NIPAM were fabricated and then its LCST was checked via DSC instrument. Fig. 2d displays the determined LCST of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels through the DSC analysis. The obtained thermogram approved the temperature sensitivity of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels and specified its LCST at about ∼ 33 °C as a sharp endothermic peak. This feature is positive in cancer therapy and may allow for its ability in local drug release after arriving at the tumor site.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology analysis by SEM and TEM apparatus

ce:section.ce:para: The DLS technique was employed to evaluate and compare the hydrodynamic diameter and surface charge density of the CQDs, CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA, and CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels to use the observed changes for showing the success in CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA nanogels synthesis and also its conjugation with D-Gal. DLS study for the CQDs exhibited narrow distribution with an average size of about 6.37 nm and the zeta potential was found to be −20.9 mV indicating the negative charge on it. The appearance of this negative charge could be the main proof for the synthesis of CQDs with surface carboxylic acid functional groups from the D-Gal. In the following, this technique was also obtained the average hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA nanogels of around 549 nm and −27.3 mv, receptively. The change in both average hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential is a good reason for the CQDs encapsulated nanogels synthesis. Additionally, functionalization of the prepared CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA by D-Gal, leading to increased zeta potential from −27.3 to −25.1 mv and average hydrodynamic diameter from 549 nm to 657 nm exhibits the binding of D-Gal to CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA nanogels through the formation of ester linkage as a result of the reaction with carboxylic groups in the shell. The obtained larger particle size in DLS analysis in comparison with the SEM and TEM, results from the performing of this test in an aqueous solution, while in both TEM and SEM imaging the thermal dehydration of the nanogels induced by the electron beam. Overall, according to our results, CQDs were successfully encapsulated into prepared CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA nanogels which were then functionalized with D-Gal.

A detailed AFM study was performed on CQDs and CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels to have a microscopic evaluation and observe the homogeneity. For AFM analysis the droplets of the dilute aqueous solution of each sample were added on a silicon substrate with a flat surface. The achieved AFM images and the corresponding height profiles are shown in Fig. 5 . The results indicate that the as-synthesized CQDs were monodisperse and quasi-spherical with a mean height of ca. 4.86 nm. CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels also displayed relatively spherical shapes which imply their structural integrity was preserved after the nanogel formation. However, the height profile significantly increased to 297 nm when the CQDs were capsulated in the nanogels form. The outcome of AFM is in agreement with the SEM characterization and certifies the truth of the synthesis process.

Lysozyme can interact with β-CD and facilitate the hydrolysis of nanogel, therefore, the change in weight of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels after dispersion in lysozyme solution was monitored at different times to evaluate it's in vitro biodegradability potential. As presented in Fig. 6 b, the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels were biodegraded in lysozyme during the 72 h. Accordingly, about 38.7% of degradation percentages were achieved after 72 h incubation of CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels in the enzymatic medium with 37 °C. Lysozymes can degrade the polysaccharides through enzymatic hydrolysis of the glycosidic linkages [52]. A similar procedure, the introduction of β-CD in the nanogels structure previously has been reported for increasing the system biodegradability [35]. After 72 h, no more degradation happened, representing that the remaining was not degradable in the lysozyme. Overall these results approve the biodegradable nature of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels that is a positive feature for its biomedicinal applications like drug delivery.

The colloidal stability of the synthesized CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels was explored in four different media to exam its potential as a stable nano-based drug carrier. The taken digital photograph at the initial time and after 72 h are presented in Fig. 6b. The initial state (0 h) of each dispersion was used as a control. Comparision of the achieved images exhibited no notable agglomeration for the dispersion of CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels in 0.1 mg/mL of concentration. Hence, based on the visual observation the designed nanosystem can be suggested as a stable nanosystem for use in biomedicinal applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro load and release study
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the DOX and MTX were selected as two commonly used anticancer drugs in chemotherapy owing to the remarkable properties of synthesized nanocarrier, to assess the efficiency of the fabricated nano-based system for load and controlled delivery of anticancer drug molecules. In fact DOX with pKa 3.8 was chosen it is be lived that DOX is able for the formation of hydrogen bonds and the charge interaction with AA and the β-CD moiety of nanogels. MTX as a hydrophobic anticancer drug with pKa 3.8, 4.8, and 5.6 was also selected for its ability the formation of hydrogen bonds and formation of an inclusion complex between the hydrophobic MTX and hydrophobic cavity of β-CD. Moreover, both of them can load on the nanogels via the formation of π–π stacking between the aromatic network of CQDs and drug molecules [5,13]. Drug loading tests were conducted separately in a two-step method; dispersion of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels in MTX (PBS, pH 7.4), and then the dispersion of MTX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels in DOX solution (PBS, pH 7.4). Subsequently, the drug loading efficiency and loading capacity were calculated using the reported Eq. (2) and Eq. (3) formula. Using these formulas the DOX and MTX loading efficiency and loading capacity respectively were calculated about 72%, 47.8%, 14.4%, and 9.56%. The π–π interaction between the aromatic ring of drug molecules and conjugated system of CQDs, encapsulation of the drug molecules into the β-CD cavity, as well as the hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions between the polar functional groups of drug molecules and CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels can be the possible reasons for the obtained drug loading percentage on CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels. Additionally, the observed difference between DOX and MTX loading (%) may be related to differences in the types and densities of functional groups for each investigated drug molecule.

In the following the release profiles of DOX and MTX from the MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels were explored at (37 °C, pH 7.4) and (40 °C, pH 5.0). The obtained in vitro cumulative release percentage of drugs vs time has been illustrated in Fig. 6c and 6d. As shown in Fig. 6c and Fig. 6d, results display that the release behaviors of both drugs were influenced by both pH and temperature stimuli. So, during the 72 h, the total release of DOX from the MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels reached about 75.31 % (40 °C, pH 5.0) and 22.37% (37 °C, pH 7.4). Interestingly, at this time, a similar release trend was observed for MTX under the mentioned conditions, 51.07 % and 18.14% of MTX released at (40 °C, pH 5.0) and (37 °C, pH 7.4) respectively. The findings suggest that the drug molecules may have some stimuli-responsive interactions with the nanogels components. π–π stacking weakening and collapsing of the nanogel network are the possible reasons for more drug releases at higher temperatures. Also, the failure of the hydrogen-bonding interaction and increasing the hydrophilicity of drugs are the other possible proof of higher drug release at acidic pH. Additionally, the illustrated release profiles exhibit that the DOX can be released faster than MTX. The finding can approve the temperature/pH-dependent feature of CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels which is one of the ideal criteria for the controlled drug delivery to the tumor environment. Therefore the fabricated system can serve as a controlled anticancer co-delivery system.

The achieved experimental drug release data were fitted to four different kinetic models; zero-order, first-order, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer-Peppas models to analyze the mechanism and kinetics of drug release (Fig. 7 and Fig. 8 ). The plots of each model were fitted linearly and the best fit was selected according to the determined regression coefficient (R2) values. In other words, the highest R2 value proposes the best correlation between the experimental release data and the kinetic model. Table 1 displays the results regarding the kinetic study. A comparison of the results shows that the DOX and MTX release at (pH 7.4, 37 °C) obeys the zero-order, whereas their releases at (pH 5.0, 40 °C) follow the Higuchi model. The calculated diffusion exponent was estimated to be 0.45–1.0, suggesting the release follows the non-Fickian mechanism.

The cytotoxicity of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels was investigated at various concentrations toward HepG2 and HUVEC cell lines to further evaluate and understand its biocompatibility against both normal and cancer cell lines. HepG2 cells overexpressed galactose receptors while the HUVEC cell line is galactose-free cells. Fig. 9 a and Fig. 9b display the viability of HUVEC and HepG2 cell lines after 48 h incubation with CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal in the concentration range from 0 to 150 µg mL−1, demonstrating desirable biocompatibility. It was detected that more than 85% of the cells remained viable following incubation for 48 h. Moreover, in the following, the inhibition effects of MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels and MTX@DOX were explored toward HepG2 cells. The obtained results display that the MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels exhibit a significant inhibition effect against HepG2 cells with the half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of 4.2 µg mL−1 compared to the free MTX@DOX with the half-maximal inhibitory concentration of 6.14 µg mL−1. This may be due to the improved cellular uptake of DOX and MTX drug molecules by the MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels in comparison with free co-drug molecules. These results showed that the fabricated biocompatible CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels after drug loading are more effective in comparison with free drug molecules.

The HepG2 cellular uptake of the MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels and MTX@DOX was studied against the HepG2 cell line; considering the intrinsic red fluorescence of DOX. For this aim, the selected cells were treated with the MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels and MTX@DOX samples, and then their uptake was monitored after 3 h. Fig. 10 a shows the distribution of the MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal and MTX@DOX in HepG2 cancer cells after 3 h. A comparison of the obtained images for both samples exhibits the increased fluorescence intensity for MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal, indicating the enhanced cellular uptake of drug molecules after loading on the fabricated nanostructure. In the following to more evaluate the output of the fluorescent microscopy, the cell uptake percentage was determined by employing the flow cytometer after 3 h incubation. The quantitative cell uptake for MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal was obtained higher than MTX@DOX which is in good accordance with fluorescent microscopy imaging outcome. Based on the above facts; higher cellular uptake and intracellular drug accumulation, the fabricated CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels with acceptable therapeutic efficacy could be suggested as an appropriate candidate for the following biomedicinal applications.

The bioimaging potential of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels was assessed based on their inherent fluorescence property. As shown in Fig. 10c, after the HepG2 cells were incubated with CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels for 0.5 h, 1 h, and 3 h, the fluorescence images of the cells were obtained employing the fluorescence microscope. An increased internalization was detected by increasing the incubation time. So, the treated cells with CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels after 3 h revealed the strong green fluorescent intensity, representing that the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal have the potential for bioimaging applications.

The changes in the nucleus morphology; healthy, necrotic, and apoptotic after 48 h incubation of the treated HepG2 cells with MTX@DOX, MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal, and CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal was evaluated employing the microscopic analysis of the DAPI stained cells. The microscopic image of the DAPI stained cells are displayed in Fig. 11 a. As it is presented the treated HepG2 cells with CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal have a similar density and normal morphology to the non-treated cells and did not show significant chromatin degradation. However, it can be detected that the treatment with both MTX@DOX and MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal led to chromatin shrinking or fragmentation and this is higher for MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal than free MTX@DOX. Therefore, it was detected that the MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal is able to induce more cytotoxicity than free MTX@DOX. Overall DAPI staining results display that the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal is a biocompatible nanocarrier and could effectively prevent the HepG2 cell growth after drug loading on it.

Flow cytometry analysis was accomplished via PI reagent to explore the effect of MTX@DOX, MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal, and CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal on HepG2 cell cycle progression (Fig. 11b). Untreated cells were used as control. The treated cells with MTX@DOX and MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal demonstrated a notable decrease of cells in the G0/G1 phase and accumulation of cells in sub G1 phases, thus showing that the MTX@DOX and MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal induced cell cycle arrest in the sub G1 phase. While the treated cells with CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal presented a similar cell cycle pattern with control. In conclusion, the results propose that the prepared biocompatible system is an excellent candidate to enhance the effect of the administered chemotropic drug molecules.

Summary in this work a highly efficient and intelligent CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels were developed and assessed for the targeted dual anticancer drug delivery to HepG2 cells. Numerous characterization techniques confirmed the successful preparation of the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels. SEM and TEM analysis obtained a spherical morphology for the fabricated nanogels which shows their suitability for drug delivery applications. Both MTX and DOX effectively were loaded on the CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels with loading percentages of about 47.8% and 72% respectively that refer to the formation of the possible interactions between the present functional groups on the structure of the nanocarrier and drug molecules. The fabricated system provided a controlled and sustained release behavior for both drugs, demonstrated by in vitro drug release studies. The outcomes of the MTT and DAPI staining tests presented the biocompatibility of the system, as well as, the higher cytotoxicity of the drug-loaded CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal nanogels with IC50 values of 4.2 μg/mL in comparison to free drug molecules. Cell uptake test results confirmed the efficient uptake of the prepared nanocarrier into the cancer cells via detecting the higher fluorescence intensity for MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal in comparison with MTX@DOX. Flow cytometry analysis showed that there was possibly subG1 cell cycle arrest for the HepG2 cells treated cells with MTX@DOX@CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal. To sum up, CQDs/β-CD/NIPAM@AA-Gal has the advantages of biodegradability, biocompatibility, colloidal stability, and stimuli-responsive drug release efficiency, and hence could be proposed as a promising candidate for the controlled and targeted intracellular dual drug delivery.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
